food_subgroup: Deep-fried prawns in salted duck egg yolk butter sauce; cooked dish with sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for salted egg yolk prawns as a composite Singapore zi char dish. Closest parent would be a fried crustacean dish node; no exact or near-exact match available in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for deep-fried prawns in sauce; no specific code for salted egg yolk prawn preparation exists.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A039T (Crustaceans, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for salted egg yolk prawn dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for salted egg yolk prawns. SNOMED CT food coverage for composite Singapore dishes is limited.

serving.description: 1 portion (~200g, prawns + salted egg yolk sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 200g of deep-fried prawns coated in salted duck egg yolk butter sauce, typically ordered as a shared zi char dish for 2–3 people at a Singapore seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Salted Egg Yolk Prawns — Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB / Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for salted egg yolk prawns. Values represent a composite cooked dish (prawns + salted duck egg yolk butter sauce) per 100g. Micronutrient values (vitamins, minerals) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) and salted duck egg yolk components to validate plausibility. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available in HPB source for this composite dish; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Seafood and Coated Fried Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted egg yolk prawns. GI estimated at ~30 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous foods: deep-fried seafood with minimal starch coating has a low GI due to negligible carbohydrate content (5g/100g, predominantly from light cornstarch coating). The high fat and protein content (butter, egg yolk, prawns) further blunts any glycaemic response. GL per 200g serving = (30 × 10g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.0 (very low). This is a defensible analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish has been published.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 280mg/100g (560mg per 200g serving); salted duck egg yolks contribute ~300mg sodium per yolk
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit salted egg yolk prawn portions to a small shared serving (≤100g personal intake) and avoid adding additional sauces, as the cumulative sodium from salted duck egg yolks and butter can approach 30–40% of the 1500mg daily sodium target recommended by the Singapore HPB for hypertensive individuals.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycaemic load (GL ~3.0 per 200g serving) but high saturated fat (10g per serving) raises long-term insulin resistance risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: The very low glycaemic load (GL 3.0) makes this dish unlikely to cause acute postprandial glucose spikes; however, the high saturated fat content (10g per 200g serving from butter and salted duck egg yolk) is a long-term metabolic concern for insulin resistance in Type 2 Diabetes patients, and consumption should be limited to occasional meals with portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 175mg/100g (350mg per 200g serving); saturated fat 10g per serving; salted duck egg yolks contain ~580mg cholesterol per yolk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease or hypercholesterolaemia should treat salted egg yolk prawns as a high-risk occasional food: the 350mg dietary cholesterol per serving (approaching the former 300mg/day limit) combined with 10g saturated fat per serving can adversely affect LDL-C levels, and those on statins or with established CVD should limit consumption to no more than once per month and choose smaller shared portions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and extreme cholesterol load; patients on corticosteroids face compounded cardiovascular risk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients on corticosteroids (which raise LDL) should limit salted egg yolk prawn dishes to once per month, as the combination of high dietary cholesterol (~350mg per serving) and saturated fat (10g per serving) compounds the cardiovascular risk already elevated by corticosteroid therapy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Egg Yolk Prawns (咸蛋虾, Xián Dàn Xiā) is one of Singapore's most iconic modern zi char dishes, popularised in the 2010s and credited with triggering a broader 'salted egg yolk craze' across Singapore's food scene. The dish consists of deep-fried tiger prawns — lightly coated in cornstarch and fried until crisp — then wok-tossed in a foamy, richly golden sauce made from mashed salted duck egg yolks, butter, curry leaves, and sliced chilli. The sauce is intensely savoury, umami-rich, and slightly sandy in texture from the egg yolk granules. Salted duck egg yolks are Chinese preserved eggs cured in salt-water brine for 30–40 days, concentrating both sodium (~300mg per yolk) and cholesterol (~580mg per yolk) to exceptionally high levels. Nutritionally, this is one of the highest-cholesterol commonly ordered dishes in Singapore: a 200g serving delivers approximately 350mg dietary cholesterol and 10g saturated fat (from butter and egg yolk combined). Sodium per 200g serving is approximately 560mg. Despite the extreme cholesterol content, current dietary guidelines have moved away from strict daily cholesterol limits for healthy adults without CVD or hypercholesterolaemia. However, for patients with existing cardiovascular disease, hypercholesterolaemia, Type 2 Diabetes with elevated LDL, or rheumatoid arthritis on corticosteroids, this dish warrants clinical caution and limitation to occasional consumption. The glycaemic load is very low (GL ~3.0 per 200g serving) due to minimal carbohydrate content, making acute postprandial glucose impact negligible. Energy per 100g is 215 kcal; protein 13g; fat 15g; carbohydrate 5g. The dish is gluten-free when prepared with cornstarch coating. It is not halal due to the use of butter (dairy) and the typical restaurant preparation context. Served as a shared dish at zi char restaurants, seafood hawker stalls, and celebratory family dinners across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are complete and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, clearly documented. Multilingual naming is complete for all mandatory Singaporean dish languages (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Ontology uses the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent code with honest gap notes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Singapore zi char dish (salted egg yolk prawn preparation) is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite cooked dish is not reported in the HPB source. All other per_100g fields are populated with values from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data (FDC ID 175180) for plausibility validation. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for salted egg yolk prawns has been published in peer-reviewed literature. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains butter (dairy) and is typically prepared in non-halal zi char restaurant settings; individual restaurants may vary. The 'salted egg yolk craze' in Singapore from ~2013 onwards saw this dish spawn numerous derivative products (salted egg yolk chips, croissants, ice cream), making it a culturally significant culinary phenomenon beyond its nutritional profile.
